System Design Concepts

Software Engineering

Distributed Systems

Networking

Web Development

6 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Understanding the Journey of a Web Request
Goal of this lesson: Build a mental model of everything that happens between pressing Enter after typing a URL and seeing a webpage.

Introduction
Open your browser.
Type:
https://www.google.comPress Enter.
Within a fraction of a second, Google’s homepage appears.
To us, it feels instantaneous. But behind the scenes, your browser, operating system, Internet Service Provider (ISP), networking equipment, Google’s infrastructure, and your browser again work together to complete dozens of operations before a single pixel is drawn on your screen.
As a backend engineer, this entire journey is your playground.
The Entire Journey at a Glance
Here’s the complete picture.
You
│
▼
Browser
│
▼
DNS Resolution
│
▼
TCP Connection
│
▼
TLS Handshake (HTTPS)
│
▼
HTTP Request
│
▼
CDN / Reverse Proxy
│
▼
Load Balancer
│
▼
Application Server
│
▼
Cache (optional)
│
▼
Database
│
▼
Application Server
│
▼
Reverse Proxy
│
▼
HTTP Response
│
▼
Browser Rendering
│
▼
You see the webpage
1) You Type a URL
Everything begins with something as simple as:
https://www.google.comThe browser immediately starts asking questions.

• Is this a valid URL?

• Which protocol should I use?

• Do I already know this website?

• Do I already have some resources cached?

• Do I know the server’s IP address?
Notice something interesting.
Computers don’t actually understand google.com.
They understand something like:
142.250.183.206So the browser’s first real problem is:
“Where is Google located?”
That leads us to DNS.

2) Find the Server (DNS)
DNS (Domain Name System) works like the Internet’s phonebook.
Instead of remembering
142.250.183.206we remember
google.comThe browser asks:
“Can someone tell me Google’s IP address?”
If the answer isn’t already cached, the request travels through DNS servers until an IP address is found.
Eventually the browser learns something like:
google.com
↓
142.x.x.xNow the browser knows where to send the request.
Not how.
Just where.

3) Establish a Connection
Knowing someone’s address isn’t enough. You still need a conversation.
The browser now creates a connection with Google’s server.
For websites, this is usually done using TCP.
TCP ensures:

• packets arrive

• packets arrive in order

• missing packets are retransmitted

• communication is reliable
Think of TCP as saying:
“Before we start talking, let’s both agree that we’re connected.”
Only after this connection exists can real communication begin.

4) Secure the Conversation
Imagine logging into your bank.
Would you want your password traveling across the Internet as plain text?
Of course not. That’s why HTTPS exists.
Before any sensitive information is exchanged, the browser and server perform a TLS Handshake.
During this process they:

• verify identities

• agree on encryption algorithms

• generate secret keys

• establish an encrypted channel
Only then does communication become private.
Without TLS, anyone on the network could potentially read your data.

5) Send the HTTP Request
Now the browser finally sends the actual request.
Conceptually it looks like this:
GET /
Host: google.comThis message says:
“Hello Google.
I’d like your homepage.”
This is the first time the browser is actually asking for data.
Everything before this point was simply preparation.

6) Reach Google’s Edge
The request doesn’t immediately arrive at Google’s application servers.
Instead, it first reaches Google’s edge infrastructure.
This layer may include:

• CDN

• Reverse Proxy

• API Gateway

• Security systems

• Firewalls
These components perform tasks like:

• blocking malicious traffic

• terminating TLS

• routing requests

• serving cached content

• logging requests

• applying rate limits
Think of it like the reception desk of a huge office building.
Visitors don’t walk directly into the CEO’s room.
They first enter reception.

7) Load Balancer Chooses a Server
Google doesn’t have one server.
It has thousands.
If every request hit the same server, it would collapse under the load.
Instead, a load balancer asks:
“Which server should handle this request?”
It chooses one healthy server from a large pool.
Request
↓
Load Balancer
↓
Server 42The next request might go to Server 18.
This distribution keeps the system fast and reliable.

8) The Application Server Thinks
The request finally reaches the application server.
This is where business logic lives.
Examples:
- Login user
- Search products
- Recommend videos
- Generate feed
- Create order
- Update profileThe application server decides:

• what needs to happen

• which services to call

• what data is required

• what response should be generated
In other words,
This is the brain of the backend.

9) Fetch Data
Most requests require data. The application server may first check a cache. If the answer already exists, great. If not, it queries the database.
Application
↓
Database
↓
ResultExamples include:

• user profile

• product information

• order history

• comments

• recommendations
The database returns the requested data.

10) Generate the Response
Now the application server has everything it needs.
It creates a response.
For example:
{
"name": "Alice",
"orders": 15
}Depending on the request, the response could be:

• JSON

• HTML

• PDF

• Image

• Video

• Error message
The response then travels back through much of the same infrastructure it came through.

11) The Browser Receives the Response
The browser now has data. But users don’t want data. They want a webpage.
So the browser begins another series of tasks:

• parse HTML

• download CSS

• download JavaScript

• request images

• build the DOM

• build the CSSOM

• execute JavaScript

• calculate layout

• paint pixels
This process is called rendering.
Interestingly, one webpage usually results in dozens or even hundreds of additional HTTP requests for images, fonts, scripts, icons, videos, and stylesheets.
The original request was only the beginning.

12) You See the Page
Finally, the browser paints pixels on the screen.
The webpage appears.
What felt like, click -> webpage
was actually an intricate dance involving networking, security, distributed systems, application logic, databases, caching, and rendering — all completed in a remarkably short time.

Putting It All Together
Let’s trace the journey one more time.
User
│
├── Types https://google.com
│
├── Browser parses the URL
│
├── DNS resolves google.com → IP address
│
├── TCP connection established
│
├── TLS handshake secures communication
│
├── Browser sends HTTP request
│
├── Request reaches CDN / Reverse Proxy
│
├── Load Balancer selects an application server
│
├── Application server executes business logic
│
├── Cache checked (optional)
│
├── Database queried (if needed)
│
├── Application generates response
│
├── Response sent back
│
├── Browser downloads remaining resources
│
├── Browser renders the page
│
└── User sees the webpage
Why So Many Components?
At first glance, this architecture might seem overly complicated. Why not let the browser connect directly to a database?
Because each component solves a specific problem:
Component | Problem It Solves
------------------| -------------------------------------------------------------------
DNS | Humans remember names, not IP addresses.
TCP | Ensures reliable communication over an unreliable network.
TLS | Protects data from eavesdropping and tampering.
HTTP | Standardizes how clients and servers communicate.
CDN/Reverse Proxy | For performance, security, and traffic management.
Load Balancer | Distributes requests across many servers to avoid overload.
Application | Implements the application's business logic.
Cache | Reduces latency & DB load for frequently accessed data.
Database | Stores persistent application data reliably.
Browser Rendering | Converts raw data into a visual experience for users. This is the central intuition of system design:
Every step exists because it solves a problem that appears as systems grow.

Key Takeaways
By the end of this article, you should remember these three ideas:

• Preparation comes before communication. The browser must find the server (DNS), establish a connection (TCP), and secure it (TLS) before sending an HTTP request.

• Large-scale systems are layered by design. Requests flow through edge infrastructure, load balancers, application servers, caches, and databases, with each layer handling a specific responsibility.

• The response journey doesn’t end at the server. The browser still has to download additional resources and render the page before the user sees anything.

What’s Next?
In my upcoming blogs, I’ll deep dive into each of these steps to get a low level conceptual view.
